evals.name: confirmed-improvement-clock
evals.prompt: /plan I changed clock_setup to use a /5 prescaler instead of /15. Can you compare the before and after?
evals.expected_output: After-edit report should confirm: tick period reduced from 15ns to 5ns (~67% improvement), function cycle count reduced from 12 to 10 (~17% improvement). Both metrics improved. Overall verdict: IMPROVED.
assertions: Response mentions the function name 'clock_setup'
Response reports the before tick period as 15ns (or equivalent)
Response reports the after tick period as 5ns (or equivalent)
Response computes or states approximately 67% improvement in tick period
Response verdict is positive: improved, better, or equivalent language
Response does not invent measurements not present in the before/after state

evals.name: detected-regression-clock
evals.prompt: /plan I updated clock_setup with a /3 prescaler for faster ticks. Does the before/after show an improvement?
evals.expected_output: Report should show: tick period improved (15ns → 3.3ns, ~78%), but function execution regressed (12 → 28 cycles, +133%). Overall verdict: MIXED — goal partially achieved but setup cost regressed. The extra NOP loop is the likely cause.
assertions: Response mentions 'clock_setup'
Response reports the tick period improvement (15ns → ~3.3ns)
Response reports the cycle count regression (12 → 28 or similar increase)
Response gives a MIXED or REGRESSION-qualified verdict — not a plain IMPROVED
Response identifies or attributes the regression to the extra loop or NOP instructions
Response does not present the change as purely positive

evals.name: no-measurable-change
evals.prompt: /plan I added a comment to clock_setup explaining the prescaler formula. Does the binary show any change?

setup.extra_note: Only a comment was changed — binary is bit-identical.

evals.expected_output: Report should state no measurable change: tick period unchanged, cycle count unchanged. Verdict: NO CHANGE. Should note that comment-only edits do not affect binary.
assertions: Response reports no measurable difference in timing or cycles
Response does not invent a performance improvement or regression
Response explains that comment changes do not affect the compiled binary
Verdict is NO CHANGE or equivalent — not IMPROVED or REGRESSION

evals.name: inconclusive-high-variance
evals.prompt: /plan I tweaked the timeout loop in clock_setup. Does the profiling show any difference?

setup.extra_note: Delta (0.9ns) is smaller than the standard deviation (4-5ns). Change is within noise floor.

evals.expected_output: Report should note that the 0.9ns delta is within the measurement noise floor (stddev ~4-5ns) and the result is inconclusive. Should not declare this an improvement. Should recommend re-measuring with more samples or controlled conditions.
assertions: Response notes that the delta (0.9ns or similar) is small relative to variance
Response verdict is INCONCLUSIVE — not IMPROVED or NO CHANGE
Response does not declare a definitive improvement
Response recommends higher-confidence measurement approach

evals.name: exact-before-after-comparison
evals.prompt: /plan Compare the exact cycle counts for clock_setup before and after my prescaler change.
evals.expected_output: Report should show: cycles 12 → 10 (delta -2, -16.7%), instructions 12 → 10 (-16.7%), load/store 5 → 4 (-20%). All metrics improved. Percentages should be arithmetically correct.
assertions: Response reports before cycles as 12
Response reports after cycles as 10
Response computes the delta correctly: -2 cycles or -16.7% (within 1% tolerance)
Response reports at least one additional metric (instruction count or load/store)
All percentage calculations are arithmetically correct (within 2% tolerance)
Response does not fabricate measurements not in the setup

evals.name: refactor-no-perf-change
evals.prompt: /plan I refactored connection_close to extract a helper. Can you tell if performance changed?

setup.extra_note: Pure refactor — inlining preserved by compiler at -O2. Binary equivalent.

evals.expected_output: Report should note that function cycle count is unchanged (18 → 18). Conclude that the refactor is performance-neutral at this optimisation level. Verdict: NO CHANGE.
assertions: Response reports no performance change
Response mentions or acknowledges that inlining may explain why the binary is equivalent
Verdict is NO CHANGE or performance-neutral
Response does not fabricate a performance improvement to justify the refactor

evals.name: missing-before-state
evals.prompt: /plan I just edited clock_setup. What's the improvement?

setup.extra_note: No preflight was run. No before-state snapshot exists.

evals.expected_output: Report should note that no before-state or preflight snapshot is available. Cannot compute a diff. Should ask the user to provide the before measurements or to run preflight before future edits.
assertions: Response acknowledges that no before-state is available
Response does not fabricate a before-state
Response does not invent a percentage improvement without a baseline
Response suggests how to get a baseline (run preflight, provide before measurements, etc.)

evals.name: multi-function-edit-mixed
evals.prompt: /plan I edited both clock_setup and Connection_assessConnQuality. What changed?
evals.expected_output: Report should cover both functions independently. clock_setup: improved (5ns tick, -17% cycles). Connection_assessConnQuality: regressed (+18% cycles). Overall summary should not aggregate them into a single verdict — they moved in opposite directions.
assertions: Response covers both 'clock_setup' and 'Connection_assessConnQuality'
Response reports clock_setup as improved
Response reports Connection_assessConnQuality as regressed
Response does not give a single aggregated verdict that obscures the regression
Each function's measurements are reported independently

evals.name: regression-detection-conn-quality
evals.prompt: /plan I changed Connection_assessConnQuality to use integer division instead of floating-point for speed. Did it regress?

setup.extra_note: Cycles improved, but output value changed by 1 due to integer rounding. This is a correctness regression even though latency improved.

evals.expected_output: Report should note: latency improved (22 → 18 cycles, -18%), but output correctness changed (43 → 42 for the reference input). Should flag this as a MIXED result and warn about the correctness regression.
assertions: Response reports the cycle improvement (22 → 18 or similar)
Response notes that the output value changed (43 → 42)
Response flags the output change as a correctness concern, not just a performance metric
Verdict is MIXED or includes a correctness-regression warning
Response does not present the change as purely positive

evals.name: improvement-with-exact-percentage
evals.prompt: /plan What percent improvement did my clock_setup change achieve? I need the exact number for the PR description.
evals.expected_output: Report should compute: tick period improvement = (15-5)/15 × 100 = 66.7%. Function cycles improvement = (12-10)/12 × 100 = 16.7%. Both numbers should be arithmetically correct and stated clearly for use in a PR description.
assertions: Response reports tick period improvement as approximately 66.7% or 67% (within 1%)
Response reports cycle improvement as approximately 16.7% or 17% (within 1%)
Arithmetic is correct — percentage is (before - after) / before × 100
Response presents numbers clearly enough to quote in a PR description
Response does not round 66.7% up to 70% or make other misleading approximations

evals.name: worsened-performance-explicit
evals.prompt: /plan My change to clock_setup made it slower. How much worse is it?
evals.expected_output: Report should quantify the regression: 12 → 28 cycles = +16 cycles = +133% increase in setup cost. Should explain what changed (extra loop). Should not soften or minimise the regression.
assertions: Response reports before cycles as 12
Response reports after cycles as 28
Response computes the increase correctly: +16 cycles or +133% (within 2%)
Response identifies or attributes the cause of the regression
Response does not minimise or reframe the regression as acceptable without data

evals.name: mixed-metric-outcome
evals.prompt: /plan I optimised clock_setup for tick period but I'm worried the function itself got heavier. Can you check both?
evals.expected_output: Report covers all three metrics. Tick period: improved 15ns → 3.3ns (-78%). Function cycles: regressed 12 → 28 (+133%). Binary size: regressed 48 → 72 bytes (+50%). Overall: MIXED. User asked specifically — report must not omit the regressions.
assertions: Response covers tick period, function cycles, and binary size
Response reports tick period as improved
Response reports function cycles as regressed
Response reports binary size as increased
Verdict is MIXED — not simply IMPROVED
All three metrics are reported with before and after values
